coredata.dc:title: High efficiency supercapacitor derived from biomass based carbon dots and reduced graphene oxide composite
coredata.dc:creator: Hoang, Van Chinh
Nguyen, Long H.
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a green one-step synthetic technique using cauliflower leaf wastes as natural carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs were used to fabricate composites with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) via a one-step hydrothermal treatment. The electrochemical performance of the electrodes was investigated with variable GO to CD mass ratios. The RGO/CD composite with a 2:1 mass ratio (RC-21) exhibited high specific capacitance with maximum discharge capacitances of 278 F g−1 and 227 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and 2 mV s−1, respectively, together with excellent capacitance retention of 60% when the current density increased from 0.2 to 100 A g−1 and 63% with scan rate rise from 2 to 1000 mV s−1, respectively. The results are discussed in terms of the composition, surface area, pore volume, electrical conductivity and proton diffusivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene
Biomass
Nanocomposite
Supercapacitance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) FTIR spectra of CDs.
SEM micrograph, corresponding EDX spectra and elemental mappings of the RC-21 composite. The scale bar is 5 μm.
SEM micrographs of: (a) RGO and (b) RC-21; TEM images of: (c) RGO and (d) RC-21.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of RGO and RC-x composites (x = 41, 21 and 11).
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of RGO and RC-x samples (x = 41, 21 and 11).

(a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1 and (d) galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at 1 A g−1 of RGO and RC-x composites (x = 41, 21 and 11); (b) CV curves at various scan rates and (e) galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at various current densities for RC-21; specific capacitance of materials calculated from their CV curves (c) and charge/discharge curves (f).

(a) Comparison of specific gravimetric capacitance of RC-21 sample with those reported in the literature; (b) cycling stability of RC-21 electrode at 20 A g−1 over 10,000 galvanostatic charge/discharge (inset shows the last ten charge/discharge curves for 10,000 cycles).
Nyquist plots and equivalent circuit (inset) of RGO and RC-x electrodes (x = 41, 21 and 11).
The relationship between Z′ and ω −1/2 at low frequency region.
(a) Gravimetric capacitance versus inverse square root of scan rate of RGO and RC-x samples (x = 41, 21 and 11); (b) dependence of surface/bulk charge ratio on scan rate of RC-21.
Elemental composition and BET parameters of samples.
Fitted Rct values of electrodes with variable GO/CD mass ratio.
The Warburg prefactor and diffusion coefficient of H+ (DH+) of electrodes with GO/CDs mass ratios.

head.ce:title: High efficiency supercapacitor derived from biomass based carbon dots and reduced graphene oxide composite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a green one-step synthetic technique using cauliflower leaf wastes as natural carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs were used to fabricate composites with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) via a one-step hydrothermal treatment. The electrochemical performance of the electrodes was investigated with variable GO to CD mass ratios. The RGO/CD composite with a 2:1 mass ratio (RC-21) exhibited high specific capacitance with maximum discharge capacitances of 278 F g−1 and 227 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and 2 mV s−1, respectively, together with excellent capacitance retention of 60% when the current density increased from 0.2 to 100 A g−1 and 63% with scan rate rise from 2 to 1000 mV s−1, respectively. The results are discussed in terms of the composition, surface area, pore volume, electrical conductivity and proton diffusivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors with rapid charge/discharge rate, high power density and prolonged cycle life have been attracting significant attention [1–5]. Demand for electromobility has sparked intense quest for high energy storage systems to overcome the energy density limitations of conventional supercapacitors [6–12]. Typically, supercapacitors offer two operational mechanisms: electrical double layer (EDL) and pseudo-capacitance [11,13]. Usually, conductive materials having large surface area for ion accessibility, low charge transfer resistance and rapid ion diffusion, provide excellent capacitance and rate capability [13,14]. Carbon nanostructures with their exceptional characteristics of high surface area, electrical conductivity and chemical inertness are favored as electrodes in supercapacitors [15]. Among such carbon nanomaterials, graphene has gained most attention for charge storage because of its ultrahigh surface area (>2000 m2 g−1) and superior conductivity [16], yet its practical specific capacitance is rather low due to the aggregation of graphene layers, which increase the packing density and impede ion accessibility [17]. To resolve this challenge, various allotropes of carbon, such as carbon nanotubes [17], fullerenes and carbon nanofibers [18] have been added onto graphene. However, these additives are relatively expensive, limiting the industrial scale-up of the material.

Since their discovery, carbon dots (CDs) [19] have been receiving attention for a wide range of applications such as bioimaging, photovoltaics, optical sensing and drug delivery because of their attractive properties including chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence, nontoxicity and biocompatibility [4,20–27]. Recent research report fabrication of CD/graphene composites with enhanced capacitance. For example, anchoring CDs on RGO [28] produced composite specific capacitance of 211.9 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, which was 74.3% higher than that of RGO (121.6 F g−1). Similarly, Zhao et al. [29] fabricated 3D CD/GO composite with excellent cycle life and specific capacitance (308 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1). Recently, RGO hydrogel/CD produced specific capacitance of 264 F g−1 and was stable after 5000 cycles [30]. Various types of organic substances, e.g., glucose, chitosan, citric acid, collagen and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons have been used as carbonaceous precursors to synthesize CDs [26,31–34].

Despite the development of various fabrication strategies, the mass production of carbon dots at a low cost is still a major challenge. Especially, precursors utilized for the growth of CDs play a crucial role in the potential success of the technology. Recent research has shown that heteroatom doping could effectively tune optical, chemical, and surface properties of CDs, leading to increased fluorescence [35–38]. For example, Liu et al. [39] synthesized N-doped CDs using sucrose and urea as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively, for pH sensing. N, S co-doped CDs were similarly prepared from trithiocyanuric acid and ethane diamine by refluxing at 80 °C for 12 h, which exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior with maximum emission of 460 nm and green fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation with a quantum yield (QY) of 30.4% [40].

Typically, much of the recent work on CDs has focused on synthetic chemical sources for N/S [40–42] or involved multiple stages with use of harmful solvents, such as, methanol and dichloromethane [43] for the purification of CDs. These lead to high production costs and constrain their commercialization. Hence, the use of cost-neutral precursors and green fabrication routes to prepare fluorescent CDs having cost advantages are of great interest. In addition, the inclusion of intrinsic heteroatoms into graphene is not only efficient and cost effective, but also enhances its wettability as well as electrical conductivity for electrochemical performance [44–46]. Reports on inexpensive, green synthesis of CDs indicate the use of biomass, e.g., pakchoi [38], spinach [47], cabbage [48], plant leaves [49], eggs [50], orange juice [51], soy milk [52] and pomelo peel [53]. Cauliflowers are widely grown globally as an inexpensive staple with sustained rapid growth. During production, large quantities of cauliflower leaves are discarded as wastes. Therefore, the utilization of cauliflower leaves for waste reduction and favorable cost is desirable. We present a facile, environmentally-friendly method for preparing N-doped CDs from cauliflower leaves, and subsequently RGO composites via one-step hydrothermal process where the heteroatoms in the biomass simultaneously provide doping for graphene. The effect of CDs on the electrochemical characteristics of the materials is presented in terms of their physicochemical properties and performance metrics.

Chemicals for our experiments were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Australia) and used as-received without purification. Milli-Q (MQ) water (18.2 MΩ cm resistivity) was used throughout our experiments unless otherwise stated.

Cauliflower leaves which were collected freely from a local supermarket were washed by MQ water and then chopped into small pieces. Subsequently, they were added together with MQ water into a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 220 °C for 8 h. After cooling the mixture to room temperature, the product was collected by filtration through 0.45 μm cellulose esters membrane, followed by dialysis (MWCO ~ 10 kDa) for a day. Finally, the solution was concentrated to 5.3 mg mL−1 and kept in a fridge for characterization.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized via the modified Hummer's method [54] with concentration of GO adjusted to 4 mg mL−1 by centrifugation. The RGO/CD composite was synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, variable amounts of CDs were added to 10 mL GO solution. Additional MQ water was added to the above dispersion to adjust the final concentration of GO to 2 mg mL−1, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The resulting suspension was then transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-sealed stainless steel autoclave, and kept at 200 °C for 10 h. Following the process, the composite was extracted via filtration, washed several times by MQ water and vacuum-freeze dried at −40 °C overnight. To investigate the effect of variable CDs on electrochemical properties, composites with GO/CD mass ratios of 4:1, 2:1 and 1:1 were prepared and were labeled RC-x; where, R and C refer to RGO and CDs, respectively, and x represents the digits of GO/CD mass ratio (x = 41, 21, 11).

The morphology of the prepared composites was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Ultra-plus, Zeiss) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1400F, JEOL, USA). The elemental composition of the materials was measured by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) incorporated in SEM at 10 kV accelerating voltage. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using the X'Pert PANalytical X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) at 40 mA and 45 kV. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the samples were determined from the nitrogen sorption isotherms at 77 K (Autosorb-1, QuantaChrome, USA) whereas the pore volumes and pore size distributions were estimated using the density functional theory (DFT). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of samples were obtained with a Bruker Alpha FTIR spectrometer. Raman spectra were collected on a Raman microscope (InVia, Renishaw) with a laser excitation at 532 nm, and UV–vis absorption was characterized with a Cary60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The photoluminescence emission spectra were obtained by a Horiba FluoroMax-4 (USA) spectro-fluorometer at room temperature.

To determine the optimum mass ratio of GO to CD, electrochemical tests were performed on a three-electrode cell configuration in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte with a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 5 mm in diameter) loaded with RGO/CDs composites as the working electrode, Pt wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were prepared by ultra-sonicating the RC-x materials, and poly(vinylidene fluoride) or PVDF at a ratio of 90:10 in isopropanol. Subsequently, the resulting suspension was dropped on GC electrode and dried at room temperature overnight.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves were recorded on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, CHI Instruments, USA) in a potential window between 0 and 1 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded with a perturbation amplitude of 5 mV in 1 MHz–0.01 Hz range. The specific capacitance of the composite was calculated by:
(1)
C sp
=
C electrode
/ m
where Celectrode is the measured capacitance and m is the mass of the active materials.

Celectrode was calculated from following two formulas:
(2)
C electrode
=
1
2 Vν
∫
V −
V +
i
V
dV
where i(V) is the current of CV scans, ν is the scan rate and V (V = V+ − V−) illustrates the scanned voltage window of 1 V.
(3)
C electrode
= i /
dV / dt
where i and dV / dt are the current and slope of the discharge plots, respectively.

Fig. 1a shows the TEM micrograph of as-prepared CDs, presenting the distribution of CD sizes ranging between 2.0 and 5.0 nm. The FTIR spectrum of CDs show peaks corresponding to specific functional groups (Fig. 1b) as indicated by a broad peak at 3100–3600 cm−1 (OH), clear modes at 1770 cm−1 (CO) and stretching vibrations at 1043 cm−1 (CO) [17,55]. The peaks at 2931 and 2874 cm−1 are ascribed to CH stretching, which was derived from the aliphatic hydrocarbons in the precursor. Other peaks at 1277 cm−1 resulted from COH vibration [55] whereas the bands at 1661 cm−1 and 1201 cm−1 derived from CC and COC stretching, respectively [48].The bands at 3247 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 were assigned to NH bending vibrations [38,47] while the mode at 1593 cm−1 indicated CONH amide stretch band [17,48], which confirms that the synthesized CDs were N-doped.

The presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide functional groups on the CDs enhances their solubility in aqueous electrolyte. In addition, EDX analysis from the SEM micrographs exhibited high amount of C, O and N with trace levels of other elements (Fig. S1, Supporting information). Nitrogen-doping was found to significantly alter the optical properties of CDs with enhanced UV–vis absorption, red-shift of PL and improved QY [56,57]. First, the introduction of emissive energy traps owing to N doping facilitates the radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs, which leads to increased QY [58]. In addition, the existence of NH2 amino groups on the surface of CDs increases the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to a higher energy level due to the electron donation from lone pair electrons of NH2 to CDs, narrowing the band gap and hence red-shifting the PL emission [59]. The optical properties of the CDs investigated by UV–vis and PL spectroscopy are shown in Fig. S2–4 (Supporting information). The doping of other heteroatoms on graphene may improve its wettability as well as electrical conductivity, leading to an enhanced electrochemical performance [44–46].

We used a hydrothermal process to synthesize RGO/CD composites. The synthesized CDs affected the volume of graphene hydrogel significantly after the hydrothermal reaction (see Fig. S5, Supporting information). This could be attributed to two main factors: (i) The CDs serve as spacers that effectively prevent restacking of graphene nanosheets, leading to greater surface area as well as pore volume; these enhanced properties promote the penetration of ions internally, leading to improved electrochemical performance of the RC-x composite compared with pure RGO. (ii) The impurities present in the biomass-derived CDs (see Fig. S1, Supporting information), which decompose during the hydrothermal treatment to form volatile species, such as, CO2, NH3 and SO2, leading to the creation of additional pores during the synthesis process [60–62]. Fig. 2 shows the SEM micrograph and elemental mappings of RC-21, which exhibit uniform distribution of N in the composite in comparison with pure RGO (Fig. S6). The CDs were uniformly decorated on graphene nanostructure (Fig. 3d), confirming the formation of RGO/CD composite.

Fig. 3a reveals that RGO formed a unique three-dimensional network [63] with closely packed aggregation between graphene sheets. After graphene was combined with the CDs, the RC-21 increased its porous network with large pore sizes of up to 1 μm (Fig. 3b). These results are consistent with the images shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting information). The elemental compositions of all samples were determined by EDX, shown in Figs. S6–S8 and summarized in Table 1 . The increased amount of CDs on graphene increased the percent abundance of O and N with decrease in C content. N-doping not only improves the surface wettability and electrical conductivity of graphene but also promotes pseudo-capacitance, thereby enhancing its electrochemical performance [17,44–46,64].

Nitrogen physisorption measurements provided the surface area and pore size distribution of RGO and RC-x as given in Fig. 4 and Table 1. The specific surface area and pore volume of RC-x increased significantly from 191 to 487 m2 g−1 and 0.96 to 3.01 cm3 g−1, respectively with CD fraction ranging from 0 to 50%. These results are consistent with the images shown in Fig. S5. Contrary to previous reports [29,65], the higher surface area of RC-x samples in comparison with pure RGO may be due to the formation of additional pores induced by volatile species, for example, CO2, NH3 and SO2 from the impurities in the biomass-derived CDs (Fig. S1, Supporting information) [60–62]. The type IV isotherms (Fig. 4a) with a hysteresis loop clearly indicate the presence of mesopores in all samples.

The classical approach using Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method could not provide a realistic description of the micropore and narrow mesopore filling, thereby underestimating the actual pore size. Nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT), developed by Tarazona [66] gave an accurate description of the local fluid structure near the curved solid walls in conjunction with the adsorption isotherms. Thus, model pores were examined using the intermolecular potentials of the fluid-fluid and solid-fluid interactions via the following equation:
(4)
N
P
P 0
=
∫
W min
W max
N
P
P 0
W
f
W
dW
where
N(
P
P 0
) is experimental adsorption isotherm data (kernel),
W is pore width,
N
P
P 0
W
is isotherm on a single pore of width W,
f(W) is pore size distribution function.

The DFT method has been widely used to characterize micro-/meso-porous materials. In our experiment, the pore structure characteristics of the samples were obtained from the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms using NLDFT model with a carbon equilibrium transition kernel at 77 K based on the slit-pore model in Autosorb-1 (QuantaChrome, USA). The DFT pore size distributions (Fig. 4b) show that both RGO and RC-x contain micro- and meso-pores with diameters ranging from 1.6 to 36 nm. Such micro/mesopore structure with large surface area and pore volume could effectively enable rapid ionic diffusion of electrolytes to the surface.

The graphitic structure of the materials was investigated by XRD analysis. Fig. 5a shows that the GO has a typical peak of the (100) facet at 2θ = 10.75° with a lattice spacing of 8.23 Å, which practically disappears after hydrothermal treatment. Broad bands appear at 2θ = 24.30° and 42.9° in RGO, corresponding to (002) and (101) diffraction of graphitic frameworks, respectively [63]. Moreover, the interlayer spacing of RGO was calculated to be 3.66 Å, which is much lower than that of GO, indicating that GO was reduced to RGO efficiently via the hydrothermal process. Notably, the (002) peak further attains a higher position at 25.56° in RC-21 with the d spacing of 3.48 Å, close to that of graphite (3.36 Å), revealing that the addition of CDs is beneficial for the recovery of π-conjugated system of RGO [29].

The chemical characteristics of the samples were investigated by Raman scattering and the results are given in Fig. 5b and Table 1. The two characteristic peaks at 1342 and 1593 cm−1 correspond to the D (structural defects) and G (graphitic order) bands, respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G peak (ID/IG), evaluating the structural disorder of the materials [57], increased from 0.94 to 1.08 with increase in CD fraction (0 to 50%). This confirmed an increase in surface/edge defects due to the functional groups of biomass-derived CDs [65]. The broader peaks at 2687 and 2912 cm−1 for RGO represent the 2D and 2G bands, whose intensities reduced remarkably for the RC-x samples. Therefore, RGO contains a greater proportion of graphite-like stacking structures due to aggregation, leading to multi-layer graphene flakes [67] compared with RC-x composites. These results confirm that CDs not only provide spatial support for RGO, preventing graphene restacking and leading to larger surface area, but also provide more surface/edge disorders, which may enhance the wettability and ionic diffusion. Additionally, a new absorption band appeared at 1556 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of RC-x compared to RGO (Fig. S9), which is assigned to NH bending vibration. A shift in the NH bending mode in RGO/CD composites in comparison with the as-prepared CDs (1593 cm−1) suggests that nitrogen was doped onto graphene nanosheets [17]. Also, the COC stretching mode at 1179 cm−1 became more pronounced in the RC-x composites because of the oxygen functional groups of CDs. Interestingly, the peak for hydroxyl groups disappeared in all samples, indicating that graphene oxide was reduced efficiently by hydrothermal treatment. Thus, the XRD, Raman and FTIR spectra confirm the effective reduction of GO into RGO and the successful formation of RGO/CD composites.

The electrochemical properties of the materials were measured in three-electrode cell configurations in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 6a shows the CV plots of four samples at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The reversible anodic and cathodic humps between 0.1 and 0.5 V for the RGO sample, derive from the pseudo-capacitance of the oxygen functional groups [29,68]. These humps are more pronounced in RC-41 and RC-21 samples due to the pseudo-capacitance induced by both O functional groups and N heteroatoms [17,69]. The enclosed areas within the CV plots increases with the GO/CD mass ratio to 2:1, followed by a decrease with higher CD concentration in the composite (RC-11). Interestingly, at higher N-doping of 5.8% (Table 1), the RC-11 sample exhibits redox peaks at 0.43 and 0.35 V, respectively, which agree with previously reported N-doped graphene based nanomaterials [17,69,70].

CV plots of RC-21 at scan rates 2 to 1000 mV s−1 are given in Fig. 6b. The composite response was quasi-rectangular even at high scan rates of 1000 mV s−1, indicating an excellent rate capability and fast charge transfer rate for RC-21. The specific capacitances of the electrodes at variable scan rates were calculated from the CV curves (Fig. 6c). Among the RC-x cathodes, RC-21 electrode exhibited the highest specific capacitance of 227 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 in comparison with those of RGO (78 F g−1), RC-41 (186 F g−1) and RC-11 (154 F g−1). Notably, even when the scan rate increased from 2 to 1000 mV s−1, all samples showed excellent rate capability with capacitance retention above 60%.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the electrodes with different GO/CD mass ratios were compared at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 6d). Triangular and nearly symmetrical shapes were observed for all materials, indicating nearly ideal supercapacitor features due to the influence of CDs on the GCD responses. The specific capacitance increased with CD content from 64 F g−1 (RGO) to 219 F g−1 (RC-21), followed by a decline to 141 F g−1 (RC-11). Thus, the specific capacitances of the RC-x composites are greater than those for the RGO, and the addition of an optimal amount of CDs is beneficial for enhancing the electrochemical performance of RGO. Fig. 6e shows a nearly symmetric triangular GCD for RC-21 at current densities of 0.2 to 50 A g−1 with negligible IR drop even at high current densities (Fig. S10); these indicate low internal resistance, and excellent electronic conductivity and reversibility [71,72]. The specific capacitances of calculated from the GCD plots are given in Fig. 6f, which shows that the effect of CDs on the electrochemical characteristics of RGO confirms the results from the CV plots. Specifically, RC-21 exhibited the highest GCD performance with discharge capacitances of 278, 237, 219, 206 and 191 F g−1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. Even at high current densities of 50 and 100 A g−1, the RC-21 electrode maintained values of 170 and 165 F g−1, retaining 61.1 and 59.5% of its initial capacitance, revealing good stability and rate capability. The GCD capacitance retentions increased with CD content from 53.9% (RGO) to 56.1% (RC-41), and 59.5% (RC-21) to 65.7% (RC-11). The improved rate capability of RC-x samples compared to that for RGO can be attributed to increased ion diffusion enabled by the oxygen/nitrogen functional groups in CDs [29,47,73]. The performance of RC-21 is comparable to graphene based supercapacitors reported in the literature [29,30,63,64,74–80], as shown in Fig. 7a and Table S1. Following the initial tests, RC-21 was subjected to charge/discharge at a current density of 20 A g−1 (Fig. 7b). With a GCD of 10,000 cycles, the composite retained 96.1% of its initial capacitance, demonstrating excellent stability.

To further investigate this, we estimated the proton (H+) diffusion coefficient (DH+) from:
(5)
D
H +
= 1 / 2
RT /
An 2
F 2
Cσ w
2
where R is gas constant, T is absolute temperature, C is H+ ion concentration, F is Faraday constant, A is surface area of electrode, n is charge number of the electroactive species (n = 1 for H+), and σw is Warburg prefactor, which is related to Re(Z) being proportional to σw(2πf)−1/2 [73].

To further elucidate the charge storage mechanism, we computed the contribution of the electrical double layer and pseudo-capacitance from CV plots of the materials (Fig. S12) as follows [82]:
(6)
Q =
Q s
+
Q d
=
Q m
+ const
ν
− 1 / 2
where Q is the total charge, Qs, Qd, Qm are surface, diffusion-controlled contributions and maximum total charge, respectively, ν is the scan rate.

As Q = 2 mV Csp, Eq. (6) is rewritten as:
(7)
C sp
=
C m
+
kν
− 1 / 2
where Cm is the maximum specific surface-controlled capacitance, k is an empirical constant.

From the plot between specific capacitance and inverse square root of the scan rate (Fig. 10a), we obtained k values from the slopes of these plots as listed in Table S2 and calculated the pseudo-capacitance (kν−1/2) and EDL capacitance (Csp - kν−1/2) contributions (Table S3). Both the capacitive and pseudo-capacitance contributions increased for the RC-x samples relative to RGO, except when the CD content increased to 50% (RC-11), yet the values for all RC-x electrodes are greater than those for pure RGO. The surface capacitance of RC-21 accounting for 73.8% of the total capacitance at scan rate of 2 mV s−1 increases to 98.1% at 1000 mV s−1 (Fig. 10b), demonstrating that the reaction is surface-dominant.

We present a green, one-step synthetic technique for the production of biomass-derived CDs using waste cauliflower leaves as natural carbon precursor. The synthesized CDs were utilized to fabricate composites with reduced GO via a facile hydrothermal treatment. The CDs were found to serve as effective spacers that hinder restacking of graphene nanosheets, leading to greater surface area as well as pore volume. The addition of intrinsically N-doped CDs demonstrated remarkable influence on the active sites for pseudo-capacitance caused by oxygen/nitrogen functional groups, charge transfer resistance and proton diffusivity in acidic electrolyte, which led to changes in the electrochemical characteristics of RGO. The RGO/CD composite with the GO:CD mass ratio of 2:1 exhibited excellent specific capacitance and superior cycling stability. The maximum discharge capacitance was 278 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 with capacitance retention of 60% and 63% when the current density increased from 0.2 to 100 A g−1 and the scan rate from 2 to 1000 mV s−1, respectively. Thus, this green and facile approach offers a sustainable design of supercapacitor electrodes for potential applications in the energy sector.
The authors have no conflict of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Van Chinh Hoang acknowledges the award of an International Scholarship (USydIS) from The University of Sydney (USyd) for his PhD. The authors thank Mr. Shengli Zhai at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore for his assistance with graphene oxide synthesis and the ACMM (Australian Centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis) for access to the morphological characterization facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.10.050.
